verses.commentary: Leaving the temple, a disciple says: Look! What massive stones! What magnificent buildings! Herod's temple was architectural marvel. Stones weighing hundreds of tons.
Jesus: Do you see these great buildings? Not one stone will be left on another; every one will be thrown down. Prophecy of temple's destruction. Fulfilled in AD 70 when Rome razed Jerusalem.
Jesus sits on the Mount of Olives opposite the temple. Peter, James, John, Andrew ask privately. Inner circle plus Andrew. The four fishermen from the beginning (1:16–20).
Tell us, when will these things happen? What will be the sign? Two questions: timing and signal. They assume temple destruction, Jesus' coming, and end of the age coincide.
Watch out that no one deceives you. First warning. Deception is the primary danger in the last days.
Many will come in my name, claiming I am he, and will deceive many. False messiahs. History is full of them. They use Jesus' name but lead astray.
When you hear of wars and rumors of wars, do not be alarmed. Such things must happen, but the end is not yet. Wars are constants, not signs. Do not confuse turmoil with the end.
Nation against nation, kingdom against kingdom. Earthquakes, famines. These are birth pains. Pain increases but birth is not yet. The end is a process, not an instant.

You must be on guard. You will be handed over to councils, flogged in synagogues. Stand before governors and kings as witnesses. Persecution predicted. Suffering for the name. But it becomes testimony opportunity.
The gospel must first be preached to all nations. Prerequisite. Universal proclamation precedes the end. Mission determines the timeline.
When arrested, do not worry beforehand what to say. Say whatever is given you at the time. It is not you speaking, but the Holy Spirit. Divine assistance in trial. The Spirit provides words.
Brother will betray brother to death, father his child. Children will rebel against parents, have them put to death. Family fracture. Micah 7:6. Loyalty to Jesus divides households.
Everyone will hate you because of me. But the one who stands firm to the end will be saved. Universal hostility. Endurance is salvation. Perseverance proves faith.

When you see the abomination that causes desolation standing where it does not belong (let the reader understand), then let those in Judea flee to the mountains. Daniel 9:27, 11:31, 12:11. Desecration of the holy place. Fulfilled in AD 70 or awaiting future fulfillment. Let the reader understand: Mark signals coded language.
Let no one on the housetop go down or enter the house to take anything. Urgency. No time to gather possessions. Flee immediately.
Let no one in the field go back for their cloak. Leave everything. Speed is survival.
How dreadful for pregnant women and nursing mothers! Vulnerable populations suffer most. Flight is harder for them.
Pray this will not happen in winter. Weather complicates escape. Prayer affects circumstances.
Days of distress unequaled from the beginning of creation until now, and never to be equaled again. Daniel 12:1. Unprecedented tribulation. History's worst moment.
If the Lord had not cut short those days, no one would survive. But for the sake of the elect, he has shortened them. Divine mercy limits the horror. The elect determine history's duration.
If anyone says: Look, here is the Messiah! or there he is, do not believe it. False announcements in crisis. Desperation breeds deception.

False messiahs and false prophets will perform signs and wonders to deceive, if possible, even the elect. Supernatural deception. Miracles do not validate truth. Even the elect are targets, but not ultimately deceived.
Be on your guard. I have told you everything ahead of time. Forewarned is forearmed. Prophecy is protection.
But in those days, following that distress, the sun will be darkened, the moon will not give its light. Isaiah 13:10, 34:4, Joel 2:10, 31. Cosmic collapse. Creation unravels.
The stars will fall from the sky, the heavenly bodies will be shaken. The universe destabilizes. Literal or symbolic? Either way, total upheaval.
At that time people will see the Son of Man coming in clouds with great power and glory. Daniel 7:13–14. Visible, glorious return. Universal sight. The suffering Son of Man returns as judge and king.
He will send his angels, gather his elect from the four winds, from the ends of the earth to the ends of the heavens. Universal ingathering. The dispersed people of God reunited. Salvation completed.
Learn from the fig tree: when its twigs get tender and leaves come out, you know summer is near. Natural sign. Observation leads to knowledge.
When you see these things happening, you know it is near, right at the door. Prophetic signs announce proximity. Recognition brings readiness.

Truly, this generation will certainly not pass away until all these things have happened. Debated. Does this generation mean the generation alive at Jesus' speaking (implying AD 70 fulfillment), or the generation that sees these signs? Either way, the promise is certain.
Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away. Ultimate authority. Creation is temporary; Jesus' words are eternal. His prophecy is more certain than the cosmos.

But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father. Stunning admission. The Son's voluntary limitation in incarnation. Date-setting is futile. The Father alone knows.
Be on guard! Be alert! You do not know when that time will come. Repeated warning. Vigilance required. Uncertainty demands readiness.

Like a man going away, leaving his house, putting servants in charge, each with assigned tasks. The one at the door must keep watch. Parable. Master absent, servants responsible. Specific duties assigned.
Keep watch because you do not know when the owner will come back: evening, midnight, rooster crow, or dawn. Four watches of the night. Any moment is possible. Constant readiness.
If he comes suddenly, do not let him find you sleeping. The danger is complacency. Sleep = spiritual lethargy.

What I say to you, I say to everyone: Watch! Universal command. Not just the four who asked, but all disciples across all time. The Olivet Discourse ends where it began: with a warning. Chapter thirteen: the Little Apocalypse. Temple destruction, tribulation, false messiahs, cosmic collapse, glorious return. Signs given, but timing withheld. The command is: Watch. Be ready. Endure. The end is certain, the date unknown. The gospel must go to all nations. Then the Son of Man comes.
